body.html: Formation of the Possessive Case
body.plain: Formation of the Possessive Case

body.html: When the noun is singular, the possessive case is formed by adding’s (which is called apostrophe s) the noun; as,

body.html: When the noun is plural, and ends in s, the possessive is formed by adding only an apostrophe; as,

body.html: Boys’ school; girls’ college; horses’ tail

body.html: When the noun is plural, and does not end in s, the possessive is formed by adding’s; as,

body.html: Men’s club; children’s park.

body.html: When a noun or title consists of several words, the possessive sign is put to the last word only; as,

body.html: When two nouns are in apposition, the possessive sign is put to the later only; as,

body.html: My, our, your, his, her, there are possessive Adjectives; they are used with nouns. Mine, ours, yours, hers, theirs are possessive Pronouns; they are used in place of nouns (not with nouns).

body.html: An Adjective is a word that modifies a noun or pronoun. Adjectives describe about the noun or pronoun. ‘This’ and ‘that’ are followed by singular nouns and ‘these’ and ‘those’ are followed by plural nouns.
body.plain: An Adjective is a word that modifies a noun or pronoun. Adjectives describe about the noun or pronoun. ‘This’ and ‘that’ are followed by singular nouns and ‘these’ and ‘those’ are followed by plural nouns.

body.html: Mistakes are often made by using 'these' and ‘those’ with singular nouns. The following sentences are incorrect:

body.plain: I don't like those sort of people.

body.plain: I don’t like that sort of people.

body.html: The words 'superior', 'inferior', 'senior', 'junior' are always used as a comparative degree but they are followed by 'to' instead of 'than':

body.html: He is junior/senior to me in age.
body.plain: He is junior/senior to me in age.

body.plain: In comparing two things, the Comparative (not the Superlative) should be used; as,

body.plain: He is the taller of the two.

body.plain: Which is the better (not best) of the two?

body.plain: Of the two evils, choose the less (not least).

body.html: When one thing is compared with every other of the same kind, take care to use 'any other' after an adjective in the Positive or Comparative degree; as,
body.plain: When one thing is compared with every other of the same kind, take care to use 'any other' after an adjective in the Positive or Comparative degree; as,

groups.instruction: Rewrite the passage with suitable articles.

items.prompt: There were once three artists. They were competing for prize. One of them painted garland of flowers. It was so beautiful that bee came and settled on picture. It thought flowers were real. Second artist painted basket of fruit so well that ox tried to eat some of it. Third artist painted curtain. When three pictures were placed before judge he tried to raise curtain, so natural did it look! This picture was declared best since it had deceived man. Other pictures had only deceived animal and insect.
items.promptHtml: There were once three artists. They were competing for prize. One of them painted garland of flowers. It was so beautiful that bee came and settled on picture. It thought flowers were real. Second artist painted basket of fruit so well that ox tried to eat some of it. Third artist painted curtain. When three pictures were placed before judge he tried to raise curtain, so natural did it look! This picture was declared best since it had deceived man. Other pictures had only deceived animal and insect.
items.answer: IV (a) There were once three artists. They were competing for a prize. One of them painted a garland of flowers. It was so beautiful that a bee came and settled on the picture. It thought the flowers were real. The second artist painted a basket of fruit so well that an ox tried to eat some of it. The third artist painted a curtain. When the three pictures were placed before a/the judge he tried to raise the curtain, so natural di0d it look! This picture was declared the best since it had deceived a man. The other pictures had only deceived an animal and an insect.

groups.instruction: Rewrite the passage with suitable articles.

items.prompt: Tiger who had become too old and weak to go hunting was lying at mouth of cave in which he lived. Jackal passed by. "Come in and talk to me," said tiger. "I am very lonely, and would be so glad to have pleasant chat with animal of your wisdom." "I fear visit may not be good for my health," replied jackal. "Oh, I am far too weak and old to hurt you," said tiger. "You can see by large number of footprints how many visitors I have had." "Yes," replied clever jackal, "I see many footprints pointing towards your cave, but I see none pointing other way."

items.answer: (b) A tiger who had become too old and weak to go hunting was lying at the mouth of the cave in which he lived. A jackal passed by. "Come in and talk to me," said the tiger. "Oh, I am far too weak and old to hurt you," said the tiger. "You can see by the large number of footprints how many visitors I have had." "Yes," replied the clever jackal, "I see many footprints pointing towards your cave, but I see none pointing the other way."
